A “nova era”, fortemente inspirada no período da Restauração Meiji no Japão, alçaria os níveis de exploração da classe operária sul-coreana a níveis alarmantes.  
Na época em que serviu ao exército imperial japonês, Pak Chung Hee recebeu bastante influência ideológica que formaria sua visão de mundo em prol de um modelo de organização social fortemente baseados na hierarquia, forte controle estatal, nacionalismo e anticomunismo. A partir desse período, os chaebols começam a despontar como a coluna vertebral da economia sul-coreana, o que garantiu que o setor mais significativo dos capitalistas sul-coreanos apoiasse decididamente o regime.  
Contra a intensificação da exploração capitalista, os operários foram buscando formas de se organizar e lutar pelos seus direitos. Denunciando as extenuantes jornadas de trabalho a que eram submetidos – oficialmente a legislação laboral previa 8 horas de diário de trabalho, algo quase nunca respeitado –, os trabalhadores começaram a enviar cartas para os mais diversos órgãos governamentais. Suas demandas eram recebidas com desprezo ou repressão pura e simples. Em 1970 – dois anos antes da instauração do regime ditatorial de Yushin – Chun Tae Il, jovem ativista operário, cometeria suicídio ateando fogo ao próprio corpo, visando chamar atenção para o problema da violação dos direitos trabalhistas na Coreia do Sul. A KCIA exercia um rígido controle contra a atividade sindical no país, permitindo apenas o funcionamento da central sindical alinhada ao regime.  
Em 1974, o governo anuncia haver derrotado uma suposta conspiração conjunta com a Coreia do Norte para a derrubada do governo, organizado por um fictício “Partido Revolucionário Popular”. Seguindo esse episódio, milhares de pessoas foram presas e torturadas pelo regime. O governo organizou um julgamento farsesco de oito líderes, que supostamente pertenciam a este grupo, e que seriam posteriormente executados em 1975. Posteriormente, ficaria provado que esse episódio foi uma farsa montada pelo regime reacionário.  
O número de protestos e revoltas contra o governo cresciam na medida em que este ia tomando formas mais reacionárias e repressivas. Entre setores importantes da pequena-burguesia urbana, crescia o descontentamento contra o regime.  
Dada a repressão sistemática e aniquilação da oposição comunista revolucionária, novas ideologias e movimentos passam a dirigir as lutas de oposição contra o regime. Esses movimentos refletiam as aspirações de um setor da média burguesia e da pequena-burguesia, excluídas de representatividade dentro do regime pró-imperialista. Já fortemente influenciadas por ideias burguesas ocidentais e distantes do socialismo e comunismo, tal grupo buscou mais voz e participação política. Ao mesmo tempo, inúmeras organizações de trabalhadores, estudantes e mulheres seriam formadas em território sul-coreano.  
No ano de 1979, novos protestos massivos voltam a sacudir a Coreia do Sul. Uma nova crise se abre no seio do regime, e Pak Chung Hee seria assassinado por um membro da KCIA. Hoje em dia, muitos economistas e sociólogos burgueses consideram o regime reacionário e anti-povo de Pak Chung Hee como responsável pelo “milagre econômico” da Coreia do Sul, e as políticas implementadas nesse período são apresentadas como “modelo” para países em desenvolvimento. Nesta época, Pak Chung Hee chegou ao poder falando em “autossuficiência econômica” e aprofundou a implementação de um programa de substituição de importações.
Pouco tempos após o assassinato de Pak Chung Hee, Chun Doo Hwan, elemento reacionário, conduz mais um golpe de Estado e dá origem a um novo regime militar na Coreia do Sul. Em 1980, seria já o chefão fascista sul-coreano, responsável principal pelos crimes contra a humanidade cometidos na cidade de Gwangju durante o mesmo ano.  
 
Conforme falamos, a presente data de 18 de maio de 2020 marca o 40° aniversário do Levante de Gwangju. Este importante evento marcou o início da crise da ditadura militar patrocinada pelo imperialismo norte-americano, exercendo uma poderosa influência na luta das massas populares sul-coreanas. O Levante foi brutalmente reprimido pelo exército sul-coreano, deixando profundas marcas na sociedade sul-coreana. O grau de combatividade demonstrado pela classe operária e os estudantes da cidade é de grande inspiração não somente para os movimentos democráticos e progressistas da Ásia, mas também noutras partes do mundo.  
A origem do Levante Popular de Gwangju remonta às manifestações massivas de estudantes, que começaram em Seul, 14 de maio, e logo se expandiram para outras regiões da Coreia do Sul, como Busan e Gwangju. Os estudantes protestavam contra a ditadura militar, reivindicavam a democratização da sociedade sul-coreana e o fim da Lei marcial. Campi universitários em todo o país foram ocupados. Sentindo as pressões do governo e preocupados com as consequências das manifestações, as lideranças estudantis cancelaram novos atos de rua. O governo não voltou atrás em suas oposições e enviou o exército para várias regiões do país, incluindo Gwangju, onde os estudantes haviam ocupado a Universidade Nacional de Cheonam. Em 16 de maio, os estudantes em Gwangju realizaram manifestações em frente ao Palácio Provincial, que foi renomeado como “Praça da Democracia” e saíram num desfile de tochas.  
Em 17 de maio, muitos líderes do movimento em Gwangju foram presos, na medida em que o exército já havia deslocado sua inteligência para atuar na cidade. Em âmbito nacional, várias lideranças políticas foram presas. Autorizado por Washington, o general John Wickman havia liberado a utilização de forças paraquedistas, que foram deslocadas, por ordens de Chun Doo Hwan, para Gwangju. Ao chegarem em Gwangju, as tropas aterrorizaram e humilharam a população da cidade.  
Em 18 de maio, reagindo às violações e repressão de que foram vítimas, os protestos em Gwangju adquirem um caráter mais radicalizado e evoluem para confrontos abertos contra o exército. Os estudantes começaram a utilizar amplamente coquetéis molotov, organizando equipes responsáveis para sua produção e arremessos durante os protestos. Pessoas dos mais variados estratos sociais começam a se juntar espontaneamente às demonstrações. Quando o proletariado entrou em cena, os militares voltaram a reagir com violência, atirando indiscriminadamente nas pessoas que participavam dos protestos. Os motoristas de ônibus e taxi também tomaram parte no movimento, usando seus veículos como escudos de proteção dos manifestantes, na medida em que pilhas de corpos iam se formando nas ruas e o resgate das pessoas era quase impossível, devido aos tiros frenéticos disparados pelos militares. As pessoas reagiam com o que tinham a seu alcance: pedras, bastões de baseball, barras de ferro, bambu, etc. Em 19 de maio, os militares começaram a usar lança-chamas contra os manifestantes, incinerando dezenas de pessoas.  
Em 20 de maio, é encontrado um corpo mutilado, o que contribuiu para enfurecer ainda mais a população de Gwangju. Milhares de pessoas se reúnem no centro da cidade e entoam a canção “Nosso desejo é a reunificação”. A marcha dos manifestantes reuniu cerca de duzentas mil pessoas e novamente foram recebidas com repressão indiscriminada. Num determinado momento, os manifestantes formaram grupos de ação que tomaram as instalações da Guarda Nacional, e vários presos foram libertados. A repressão prosseguia violentamente.  
Em 21 de maio, a luta se intensifica com a entrada dos operários da fábrica Asia Motors. Muitos moradores de Gwangju se aproximaram da fábrica para pedir que os operários se engajassem na luta. Os trabalhadores da fábrica aceitaram o pedido, pararam a produção e forneceram vários veículos para os manifestantes. Cerca de 414 veículos militares foram doados para os manifestantes. Em seguida, uma coluna de operários, dirigindo os próprios veículos que haviam produzido, chega em Gwangju para participar do levante. Num determinado momento, os manifestantes formaram grupos de ação que tomaram as instalações da Guarda Nacional e vários presos foram libertados. Trabalhadores fabris, pescadores e mineiros de localidades próximas de Gwangju também se dirigiram à cidade, trazendo com eles armas e munições.  
As milícias populares – chamadas de “Exército Civil” – tomaram conta da cidade, expulsando os militares, desempenhando um papel de “liderança” do levante. Com os militares fora da cidade, Gwangju se converteu muma espécie de “comuna”, com o surgimento de formas bastante embrionárias de novas relações sociais. O espírito de solidariedade que se criou na sociedade durante os dias do levante é algo bastante comentado e analisado em estudos sobre os acontecimentos em Gwangju. Kim Il Sung, então presidente da República Popular Democrática da Coreia (Coreia do Norte), chegou a afirmar que o Levante Popular de Gwangju “foi a primeira sublevação massiva do mundo depois da Comuna de Paris”. [4]  
Precisamos ressaltar que o Levante Popular de Gwangju, apesar de ter sido classificado pelos militares sul-coreanos como uma “rebelião armada comunista organizada pela Coreia do Norte”, teve na espontaneidade uma de suas marcas principais. Como sempre, a bandeira do anticomunismo foi utilizada para justificar o massacre e os ataques contra as massas populares.  
No dia 27 de maio, o governo desloca as tropas especiais que ficam estacionadas na Zona Desmilitarizada da Coreia (fronteira com a RPDC) para ocupar a cidade de Gwangju e esmagar militarmente a resistência armada das milícias populares formadas pelos estudantes e operários. 
 
O deslocamento de tropas para a cidade de Gwangju contou com a aprovação do general John Wickman, então comandante das Forças de Comando Combinadas da República da Coreia e Estados Unidos. Os politiqueiros e ideólogos do imperialismo norte-americano davam declarações públicas “condenando” qualquer violação dos direitos dos manifestantes, ao mesmo tempo em que, por debaixo dos panos, conspiravam e davam autorizações ao regime militar reacionário.  A gigantesca maior parte do armamento, munição e veículos utilizados na repressão contra o povo da cidade foram fornecidos ao Estado fantoche sul-coreano pelo imperialismo norte-americano. A repressão ao Levante Popular também contou com aval de Jimmy Carter, então Presidente dos Estados Unidos, Zbigniew Brzezinski, Conselheiro de Segurança Nacional e Richard Holbrooke, Secretário de Estado Adjunto.  A grande preocupação dos ideólogos do imperialismo era o de manter a “estabilidade” do regime fascista e, como não poderia deixar de ser, combater o fantasma do comunismo. Jimmy Carter, durante sua campanha eleitoral, chegou a defender a ideia de retirar as tropas norte-americana estacionadas na península coreana, mas passou à história como um personagem que colaborou com um dos episódios mais sangrentos cometidos pelo regime sul-coreano e o imperialismo. 
 
Como falamos anteriormente, o Presidente Kim Il Sung chegou a comparar o Levante Popular de Gwangju com a Comuna de Paris. De fato, existem várias semelhanças entre os dois acontecimentos. Os dois surgiram como insurreições armadas urbanas, que estabeleceram mecanismos novos de participação popular e desbarataram os mecanismos e forças repressivas do Estado burguês. Apesar de no caso da Comuna de Paris o caráter operário ser muito mais enfático, também em Gwangju os protestos contaram com participação essencial dos trabalhadores, que inclusive introduziram uma dinâmica mais combativa e radicalizada ao movimento.  
As diferenças residem no fato de, politicamente, o movimento em Gwangju ser muito mais “confuso” e espontâneo do que a Comuna de Paris. Um participante do movimento em Gwangju chegou a afirmar que muitos participantes do Levante Popular tinham a ingênua esperança de que os Estados Unidos viriam salvar os estudantes, o que demonstra uma certa incompreensão da dinâmica política internacional e da própria situação em que estavam inseridos. Outro fato que diferencia Gwangju da Comuna de Paris é o fato de a primeira ter tido uma duração ainda mais incipiente que a segunda. O Levante Popular de Gwangju durou apenas dez dias, e apesar de ter instituído um comitê civil que começou a deliberar sobre os assuntos políticos da cidade, desbaratado os aparatos repressivos do Estado burguês em prol de uma nova estrutura militar surgida do povo, ainda não poderia ser considerado um novo poder na correta acepção do termo, dado o caráter bastante embrionário dessas novas formas organizativas. Os acontecimentos em Gwangju revelam, uma vez mais, que sem um movimento cujo núcleo principal seja a classe operária, e que além disso, esteja armado de uma ideologia orientadora correta, vitórias maiores são insustentáveis. Contudo, não podemos negar a importância histórica do Levante Popular de Gwangju e da experiência histórica das massas populares sul-coreanas. O Levante Popular de Gwangju mostrou que as forças do inimigo podem sim ser enfrentadas com decisão. O Levante também desnudou o verdadeiro caráter do regime militar reacionário e do imperialismo norte-americano. O exemplo dos milhares de combatentes que tombaram lutando contra a opressão reacionária serviu de impulso para a derrubada da ditadura militar fascista em 1987.  
 
[1] Anna Louise Strong. In North Korea: First-Eye Report. Soviet Russia Today, New York: 1949  
[2] Bruce Cummings. The Korean War – A History. The Modern Library, New York: 2011 
[3] George Katsiafikas. Asia’s Unknown Uprisings – Volume I: South Korean Social Movements in the 20th Century. PM Press, Oakland: 2012  
[4] Kim Il Sung. Selected Works – Volume 35. Foreign Language Press, Pyongyang: 1989
Mestre em Filosofia Marxista pela Universidade Normal de Pequim
en_title: Gwangju Levant: brief history of the resistance of the South Korean people
en_content:
- On May 18, 1980, the heroic South Korean people took weapons to oppose the slaughter of the military-fascist regime of Chun Doo Hwan, sponsored by Yankee imperialism. Under the pretext of “combating communism” and the so-called “pro-Korea Rebelions of the North”, the Chun Doo Hwan regime sent its troops, tanks and even military planes to occupy the campuses of the main universities of South Korea, repressing the militant South Korean student movement that mobilized in defense of the full democratization of South Korea, by the revocation of the fascist
In the city of Gwangju (province of Cholla do Sul), the hordes of the regime of Chun Doo Hwan, insanas and unloading the most animalistic hatred through arrests, beats of students to death, indiscriminate fuzilaments against the common population, invasion of homes and properties, rapes, forced abortions against pregnant women, among other barbarities, the fascist The masses of Gwangju do not passively accept the humiliations and daily murders of their parents, mothers, children and loved friends. From protesters who had already served in the South Korean reactionary army, it was not difficult for the people to invade mass deposits of government weapons – since the troops were mobilized in their overwhelming majority for street repression –, surrendering and executing officers, expropriating weapons for the formation of what would become the Civil Army.
The armed struggle in Gwangju would last about ten days. In the meantime, the armed masses managed to expel the troops of Chun Doo Hwan and maintain control of the city, building a headquarters in what was formerly Gwangju City Hall. Being the masses of Gwangju, however, isolated, without a unified political perspective among the different sectors of the popular movement, without the support and organization of the masses in the rest of the country and without the due military experience, the siege of the regime of Chun Doo Hwan over the city, blocking it and exhausting it by cutting supplies of food, fuels and other supplies, ends by imposing a defeat to the heroic resistance of Gwang
It seems curious to use terms like “fascism”, “deaths”, “spancaments”, “fight for democracy” or too much to characterize the South Korean regime, at a time when all these words are attributed to North Socialist Korea. It is possible that the overwhelming majority of the Brazilian left, in almost its entirety, had never heard of what was the Levant of Gwangju, a true Paris Commune of the South Korean people, who stood up in arms against their exploiters and oppressors. We are part of a generation which has been denied the right to know the history of the struggle of the peoples of the world, the wars of resistance and the truth about who really stands beside fascism, and who puts himself for democracy and social progress. This is particularly true in the case of Gwangju, the forgotten massacre of South Korean fascism.
Moreover, the struggle of the South Korean people is not limited to the Levant of Gwangju. On the contrary, since its founding in the year 1948 by the criminal occupation of the United States, the southern Peninsula was and is the stage of intense popular struggles against criminal political repression, which accompanies all its history.
Therefore, on the date of the 40th anniversary of the beginning of the Gwangju resistance, we think it is pertinent, as a way of honoring the South Korean masses in struggle, to trace a brief history of their resistance against American imperialism and the local reaction. We hope to help Brazilians further develop their internationalist feelings towards national liberation struggles.

Before entering the Gwangju Levant, we will draw a presentation of the development of the neo-colonial regime in South Korea, which lived for years under the tutelage of a fascist military regime supported by the United States.
Since the liberation of Korea from the yoke of Japanese imperialism in 1945, the southern part of the peninsula lives under the military occupation of the United States, which, in the context of the Cold War, promoted anti-communism and fascism, as a way of containing the rise of the workers' and popular struggles of the South Korean people, and that inspired by the example of what occurred in North Korea, demanded the construction of a democratic sovereign state.
After a long war of liberation against Japan, the north of the Korean peninsula was liberated from Japanese imperialism by the joint actions of the People's Revolutionary Army of Korea, led by Kim Il Sung, and the Soviet Red Army. The United States hoped that, with the surrender of Japan, they could thus occupy the entire Korean territory. Seeing the fulminant advance of the guerrilla forces, together with the Red Army, the American imperialists proposed the establishment of Parallel 38, which cut the Korean peninsula in the middle and would soon sacrament the historical division between “North Korea” and “South Korea”.
The United States would park its troops in the south of the peninsula only a few weeks after the liberation of the country, without having provided any effective contribution to the expulsion of Japanese forces from Korea. One of the first measures taken by the Yankee military was the forced dissolution of the popular committees, putting ahead of political power figures linked to the former colonial administration and even Japanese imperialism. In the northern part of the peninsula, the democratic, anti-imperialist and anti-feudal revolution advanced with impetus, serving as a solid basis for the establishment of a unified, democratic and prosperous state.
It is important to note that the United States, since they arrived in the south of the Korean peninsula, has never played any minimally progressive role. Before the arrival of U.S. occupants, the South Korean masses had established the People's Republic of Korea: an attempt to build a new state, which was supported by local popular committees. The policy of repression and suppression practiced by the United States in South Korea was completely different from that applied by the Soviets in the North, where the popular committees were recognized, and the activity of the democratic parties were carried out normally.
Upon arriving in the country, the United States soon tried to end this experience, dissolving the People's Republic of Korea, arresting and pursuing its leading leaders.
Since the beginning, the United States has tried to impose on the country a military and neo-colonial type administration, controlling South Korea politically, culturally and economically. In 1946, to try to legitimize their military administration, they brought from the United States that would be the first dictator of the future puppet republic: Syngman Rhee (or Ri Sin Man). Syngman Rhe was a historically pro-American figure, which had been exiled in the United States in 1904, and was later appointed as president of a self-proclaimed "Democratic Assembly of South Korea", in 1947, would be renamed "Interina Legislative Assembly".
During a certain period, the United States the Soviet Union held several talks to establish a solution to the problem of the division of Korea. According to journalist Anna Louise Strong:
“For two years, these conversations only contributed to increasing bitterness. The Americans insisted on including in the Provisional Government the pro-Japanese collaborators and the exiles who returned. The Russians refused. The Russians insisted on including representatives of trade unions, peasant organizations and similar organizations. The US didn't want to hear about it.
Finally, the Soviets ended up proposing that both countries withdraw from Korea – which was fulfilled by the Soviet party in December 1948 – a proposal obviously rejected by the American imperialists.” [1]
In 1948, the United States promoted a farsighted electoral process, which would later culminate in the founding of the “Republic of Korea”, a puppet state controlled by the United States. The United States, still in 1947, took advantage of its hegemony in the United Nations to approve a resolution that would ensure the election. In this resolution, the so-called United Nations Temporary Committee for Korea emerged, which would be responsible for organizing the electoral farce. The northern part of the Korean peninsula obviously did not take part in the process.
The holding of separate elections was the subject of intense recourse by broad sectors of the Korean masses, which saw to the extent an attempt to perpetuate the division of the peninsula. Even before the elections were held, there was already the understanding that the United States and the South Korean reactionaries aimed at holding elections of such a kind. Throughout the territory of South Korea, revolts against U.S. imperialism broke out.
On May 1, 1947, vast sections of the population of Jeju Island went out to the streets to denounce manoeuvres to hold separate elections. The forces of repression fired against the unarmed population, resulting in the death of about six people. Because it is an island situated south – therefore a separate territory from the mainland part of Korea – Jeju had its own political dynamics. According to historian Bruce Cumings:
" Jeju's effective political leadership, until early 1948, was exercised by powerful left-wing popular committees, which first emerged in August 1945, and continued to operate under American occupation (1945–1948). The American occupation preferred to ignore Jeju, rather than to give reason to the committees; that pointed out a formal continental leadership, but let the people of the island manage their own affairs.” [2]
The manoeuvres of the South Korean reactionaries and the American imperialists to set up a separate country, by carrying out far-reaching elections, triggered a deep hatred in the population of Jeju, which led an armed struggle against US imperialism and its puppets. Largely run by the South Korean Labour Party, the island's masses launched armed confrontations, set fire to voting centers and police stations. The working class carried out important strikes, which drew attention for their radicality.
To suppress the demonstrations of the Jeju population, the American imperialists recruited for dirty work the “services” of the so-called “North Youth League”, paramilitary organization of fascist type, formed in its great measure by reactionary elements that escaped from North Korea. The North Youth League was known for its cruelty to the population. So the historian George Katsiaficas said: “These unpaid murderers lived from the looting of war, raping and murdering from village to village, subjecting the island’s native defenses – particularly women – to torture and humiliation.” [3]
The People's Levant of Jeju was one of the first major revolts of the South Korean people against the oppression of US imperialism and South Korean reactionaries. The People's Levant of Jeju was drowned in a sea of blood, leaving between 30,000 and 60,000 dead, with about 40,000 people seeking exile in Japan. At this time table, there are other important uprisings that can also be remembered, such as the Yo Su Levant. In the port city of Yo Su, a contingent of troops waiting to repress the people of Jeju refused to serve as a cannon bush in the massacre of their countrymen, and rebel against the government, passing to the armed struggle. As we can see, the South Korean people, from the beginning, resisted bravely against the machinations of American imperialism. Only after several repressive campaigns and a real massacre against the population, the Republic of Korea – puppet state until the present time in the southern part of the peninsula – could be founded. During the Korean War, numerous massacres would also be committed by the puppet regime in collusion with US imperialism, so that we could write dozens of articles about each of them.

The first stage of the Republic of Korea was commanded by Syngman Rhee. After the fraudulent elections held on May 10, 1948, Syngman Rhee would rule South Korea from this year to 1960. During the time he was in power, Syngman Rhee headed the country with an iron hand, chasing thousands of patriots who fought against the domination of American imperialism. One of the most emblematic cases of political repression against pro-reunification leadership was the arrest (1956) and execution (1959) of Cho Pong Am, who had been a candidate for the presidency of South Korea by the Progressive Party and received two million votes in the elections held in 1956. Cho Pong Am, like most political opponents to the Marionete regime, would be accused of “Spy of North Korea”.
From the economic point of view, the country was also placed under full dominion and dependence on the US financial “auxiliary” which in 1953 represented 14% of the country’s Gross National Product, and which in 1957 would reach approximately 23%. A significant part of this amount stood in the pockets of a new class of bureaucratic capitalists that formed, as well as the correligionaries of Syngman Rhee and his flock. Corruption reached gigantic levels and contributed to increasing popular dissatisfaction against the regime, which never enjoyed real legitimacy among the popular masses. On March 11, 1960, Masan's population goes to the streets to protest the fraudulent elections held by Syngman Rhee. On March 15, 1960, such protests spread to other cities in South Korea and are violently repressed. The repression against the movement only caused the mass rebellion to increase in adherence. In Seoul, protesters surrounded and set fire to government buildings, the Liberal Party (which was in the government) and the Anti-Communist Youth Association of the Republic of Korea. Although the demonstrations initially directed against the Syngman Rhee government, there was a strong anti-imperialist feeling behind the student's motivations. The U.S. State Department, hypocritically, was aware of having the country’s image linked to the government of Syngman Rhee, published a note requiring the South Korean government to take “necessary and effective actions to protect the democratic right of expression, assembly and freedom of the press,” as well as having “criticized” the government’s repressive measures.
Using a tactic that would be repeated in other episodes – such as in the People's Levant of Gwangju of 1980 –, the United States sought to pass to the South Korean people and international public opinion an image of "distancing" in relation to the Syngman Rhee government, as if the current reactionary regime in South Korea was not itself a Yankee creation. As noted by an editorial of the People’s Diary, published on April 25, 1960, “without the North American bayonets, the Syngman Rhee regime would not have lasted a day at all.” Syngman Rhee eventually resigned, ending his unfortunate U.S. lacquering trajectory in an exile in Hawaii.

After the fall of Syngman Rhee, South Korea lived a very short period of bourgeois “democracy”. The administration of the puppet state passed into the hands of more liberal sectors of the South Korean ruling classes, the state was reorganized into parliamentary bases and Chang Myon was elected Prime Minister of the country. On 16 May 1961, Chang Myon was the victim of a coup d'état led by reactionary Pak Chung Hee. The coup inaugurates a new phase in South Korea's political life, which would also be marked by the brutal exploitation of the workers and repression of the mass movements, especially those of a revolutionary character. Pak Chung Hee establishes a military dictatorship of fascist type.
Pak Chung Hee was a former Japanese collaborator. During his youth, he joined the Imperial Japanese Army and studied at the Tokyo Military Academy. His Japanese name was Okamoto Minoru. Still at the beginning of his government, he installed a so-called “revolutionary court” which was in charge of arresting and executing some names from the previous period. The coup d'état perpetrated by Pak Chung Hee sought to present itself to the masses as the real culmination of the spirit of the April 1960 revolts that overthrew Syngman Rhee. In the beginning, he adopted a hostile stance to the so-called chaebols, South Korean state conglomerates that had begun to thrive still time of Syngman Rhee, thanks to his policy of “import substitution”. Chaebols were seen by South Korean public opinion as sources of corruption. Pak Chung Hee, along with his political collaborator Kim Jong Pil, would create KCIA, an anti-communist espionage agency, which would be known for international kidnappings of South Korean teachers and students living abroad. Promotes an open hostility policy against the socialist countries. The American imperialists assessed that under Pak Chung Hee, Korea would be advancing correctly.
With the Vietnam War, the United States' ability to finance the South Korean economy is weakened (at the time 80% of fixed capital investments came from the United States). Thus, Par Chung Hee proposed, with Yankee support, the normalization of relations and a greater approximation with Japanese imperialism. In March 1964, in front of the negotiations between Japan and South Korea, the South Korean popular masses again stand up, openly shouting “Death to Japanese imperialism!”.
In June 1964, the reactionary government declared martial law and the United States Combined Forces Command authorized the displacement of two combat divisions to suppress the protests. At the same time, the puppet government approves the sending of South Korean soldiers to fight alongside the Yankee invaders in the Vietnam War. Chun Doo Hwan, who would become a central figure in the suppression of the Popular Levant of Gwangju, served as commander of a division of the South Korean puppet army in Vietnam. Participation in the Vietnam conflict was also an opportunity for South Korean reactionaries to profit from the sufferings and massacres of the Vietnamese people. South Korean companies of supply transport, civil construction and entertainment obtained gigantic profits during this period.
To deepen its reactionary domination under the puppet political regime of South Korea, Pak Chung-hee promotes in October 1972 an auto coup, dissolving the National Assembly and drafting a new constitution, which would be known as the Yushin Constitution. The constitution of Yushin represents an even greater closure of a regime that was already brutally dictatorial and fascist. The “new era”, strongly inspired by the Meiji Restoration period in Japan, would raise the levels of exploitation of the South Korean working class at alarming levels.
At the time when he served the Imperial Japanese army, Pak Chung Hee received much ideological influence that would form his worldview for a model of social organization strongly based on hierarchy, strong state control, nationalism and anti-communism. From that time on, the Chaebols begin to despont as the vertebral column of the South Korean economy, which ensured that the most significant sector of the South Korean capitalists decisively supported the regime.
Against the intensification of capitalist exploitation, the workers were seeking ways to organize themselves and fight for their rights. Reporting the extenuating working days to which they were submitted – officially labor law provided 8 hours of workday, something almost never respected –, the workers began sending letters to the most diverse government agencies. Their demands were met with contempt or pure and simple repression. In 1970 – two years before the dictatorial regime of Yushin – Chun Tae Il, a young worker activist, would commit suicide by firing his own body, aiming to draw attention to the problem of the violation of labor rights in South Korea. KCIA exercised a strict control against trade union activity in the country, allowing only the functioning of the trade union center aligned with the regime.
In 1974, the government announced that it had defeated an alleged joint conspiracy with North Korea for the overthrow of the government, organized by a fictional “People’s Revolutionary Party”. Following this episode, thousands of people were arrested and tortured by the regime. The government organized a pharass trial of eight leaders, who allegedly belonged to this group, and would later be executed in 1975. It would later prove that this episode was a farce set up by the reactionary regime.
The number of protests and revolts against the government grew as it was taking more reactionary and repressive forms. Among important sectors of the urban petty-bourgeois grew discontent against the regime.
Given the systematic repression and annihilation of the revolutionary communist opposition, new ideologies and movements begin to direct the opposition struggles against the regime. These movements reflected the aspirations of a sector of the middle bourgeoisie and the petty-bourgeois, excluded from representation within the pro-imperialist regime. Already heavily influenced by Western and distant bourgeois ideas of socialism and communism, this group sought more voice and political participation. At the same time, numerous workers, students and women organizations would be formed in South Korean territory.
In 1979, new mass protests shake South Korea again. A new crisis opens within the regime, and Pak Chung Hee would be murdered by a KCIA member. Nowadays, many bourgeois economists and sociologists consider the reactionary and anti-people regime of Pak Chung Hee as responsible for the “economic miracle” of South Korea, and the policies implemented in this period are presented as “model” for developing countries. At this time, Pak Chung Hee came to power speaking of “economic self-sufficiency” and deepened the implementation of a import replacement program.
Shortly after the assassination of Pak Chung Hee, Chun Doo Hwan, reactionary element, leads another coup and gives rise to a new military regime in South Korea. In 1980, he would be the South Korean fascist chief, chiefly responsible for the crimes against humanity committed in the city of Gwangju during the same year.

As we speak, the present date of May 18, 2020 marks the 40th anniversary of the Gwangju Levant. This important event marked the beginning of the crisis of the military dictatorship sponsored by US imperialism, exerting a powerful influence on the struggle of the South Korean popular masses. The Levant was brutally repressed by the South Korean army, leaving deep marks in South Korean society. The degree of combativity demonstrated by the working class and the students of the city is of great inspiration not only for the democratic and progressive movements of Asia, but also in other parts of the world.
The origin of the Popular Levant of Gwangju dates back to massive student demonstrations, which began in Seoul, May 14, and soon expanded to other regions of South Korea, such as Busan and Gwangju. The students protested against the military dictatorship, claimed the democratization of South Korean society and the end of martial law. University campuses across the country have been occupied. Feeling the pressures of the government and concerned about the consequences of the demonstrations, the student leaders canceled new street acts. The government did not go back to its opposition and sent the army to various regions of the country, including Gwangju, where the students had occupied the National University of Cheonam. On May 16, students at Gwangju held demonstrations in front of the Provincial Palace, which was renamed the “Praça da Democracia” and left in a torch parade.
On May 17, many leaders of the Gwangju movement were arrested, as the army had already shifted its intelligence to act in the city. At national level, several political leaders were arrested. Authorized by Washington, General John Wickman had released the use of paratrooper forces, which were displaced by orders from Chun Doo Hwan to Gwangju. Upon arriving in Gwangju, the troops terrorized and humiliated the city's population.
On May 18, reacting to the violations and repression that were victims, protests in Gwangju acquire a more radicalized character and evolve into open clashes against the army. Students began to widely use molotov cocktails, organizing teams responsible for their production and pitches during protests. People of the most varied social strata begin to spontaneously join the demonstrations. When the proletariat entered the scene, the military reacted violently, indiscriminately shooting the people who participated in the protests. Bus and taxi drivers also took part in the movement, using their vehicles as protesters' shields, to the extent that piles of bodies were forming in the streets and the rescue of people was almost impossible, due to the frenetic shots fired by the military. People reacted with what they had at their fingertips: stones, baseball bats, iron bars, bamboo, etc. On May 19, the military began to use flamethrowers against protesters, incinerating dozens of people.
On May 20, a mutilated body is found, which contributed to further enraging the Gwangju population. Thousands of people gather in the city centre and sing the song “Our desire is reunification”. The march of the protesters gathered about two hundred thousand people and again were received with indiscriminate repression. At a certain time, the protesters formed action groups that took the National Guard facilities, and several prisoners were released. Repression continued violently.
On May 21, the struggle intensifies with the entrance of the workers of the Asia Motors factory. Many residents of Gwangju approached the factory to ask the workers to engage in the struggle. Factory workers accepted the request, stopped production and provided various vehicles for protesters. About 414 military vehicles were donated to protesters. Then a column of workers, driving the vehicles they had produced, arrives in Gwangju to participate in the uprising. At a certain time, protesters formed action groups that took the National Guard facilities and several prisoners were released. Plant workers, fishermen and miners from localities near Gwangju also went to the city, bringing with them weapons and ammunition.
Popular militias – called the “Civil Army” – took over the city, expelling the military, playing a role of “leadership” of the uprising. With the military outside the city, Gwangju became a mume kind of “commune”, with the emergence of quite embryonic forms of new social relations. The spirit of solidarity that was created in society during the days of the uprising is somewhat commented and analyzed in studies on events in Gwangju. Kim Il Sung, then president of the Democratic People's Republic of Korea (North Korea), said the People's Levant of Gwangju “was the first massive uprising of the world after the Paris Commune.” [4]
We need to emphasize that the Popular Levant of Gwangju, although it was classified by the South Korean military as a “Communist armed council organized by North Korea”, had in spontaneity one of its main brands. As always, the anti-communism flag was used to justify the massacre and attacks on the masses.
On May 27, the government shifts the special troops stationed in the Demilitarized Zone of Korea (front with the DPRK) to occupy the city of Gwangju and militarily crush the armed resistance of the popular militias formed by the students and workers.

The displacement of troops to the city of Gwangju was approved by General John Wickman, then commander of the Combined Command Forces of the Republic of Korea and the United States. U.S. imperialism’s political and ideologists gave public statements “condemning” any violation of the rights of protesters, while, under the cloths, they conspired and gave authorizations to the reactionary military regime. The gigantic majority of the armaments, ammunition and vehicles used in the repression against the people of the city were provided to the South Korean puppet state by US imperialism. The repression of the Popular Levant was also endorsed by Jimmy Carter, then President of the United States, Zbigniew Brzezinski, National Security Advisor and Richard Holbrooke, Assistant Secretary of State. The great concern of the ideologists of imperialism was to keep the “stability” of the fascist regime and, as it could not fail to be, to fight the ghost of communism. Jimmy Carter, during his electoral campaign, defended the idea of withdrawing U.S. troops stationed in the Korean peninsula, but went to history as a character who collaborated with one of the bloodiest episodes committed by the South Korean regime and imperialism.

As we spoke earlier, President Kim Il Sung came to compare the Popular Levant of Gwangju with the Paris Commune. In fact, there are several similarities between the two events. The two emerged as urban armed insurrections, which established new mechanisms of popular participation and dismantled the mechanisms and repressive forces of the bourgeois state. Although in the case of the Paris Commune the workers' character was much more emphatic, in Gwangju the protests also had an essential participation of the workers, who even introduced a more combative and radicalized dynamic to the movement.
The differences lie in the fact that, politically, the movement in Gwangju is much more “confident” and spontaneous than the Paris Commune. A participant of the movement in Gwangju affirmed that many participants of the Popular Levant had the naive hope that the United States would come to save students, which demonstrates a certain incomprehension of the international political dynamics and the very situation in which they were inserted. Another fact that differentiates Gwangju from the Paris Commune is the fact that the first had an even more incipient duration than the second. The People's Levant of Gwangju lasted only ten days, and despite having instituted a civil committee that began to deliberate on the political affairs of the city, disbarred the repressive apparatus of the bourgeois state for a new military structure emerged from the people, could still not be considered a new power in the correct meaning of the term, given the very embryonic character of these new organizational forms. The events in Gwangju reveal, once again, that without a movement whose main core is the working class, and that besides, it is armed with a correct guiding ideology, greater victories are unsustainable. However, we cannot deny the historical importance of Gwangju Popular Lift and the historical experience of the South Korean popular masses. The Gwangju People's Lift showed that the enemy's forces can be faced with decision. The Levant also denounced the true character of the reactionary military regime and US imperialism. The example of the thousands of fighters who fought against reactionary oppression served as a boost to the overthrow of the fascist military dictatorship in 1987.

[1] Anna Louise Strong. In North Korea: First-Eye Report. Soviet Russia Today, New York: 1949
[2] Bruce Cummings. The Korean War – A History. The Modern Library, New York: 2011
[3] George Katsiafikas. Asia’s Unknown Uprisings – Volume I: South Korean Social Movements in the 20th Century. PM Press, Oakland: 2012
[4] Kim Il Sung. Selected Works – Volume 35. Foreign Language Press, Pyongyang: 1989
Master in Philosophy Marxist by the Normal University of Beijing
en_subtitle: On May 18, 1980, the heroic South Korean people took weapons to oppose the slaughter of the military-fascist regime of Chun Doo Hwan, sponsored by Yankee imperialism

title: As Ilhas Chagos, a história de um crime contra a humanidade
subtitle: Entre 1966 e 1973, toda a população desse arquipélago no Oceano Índico foi deportada de sua terra natal para permitir a instalação de uma base militar dos EUA
content:
Há mais de meio século, o povo chagossiano vem travando uma luta contra a arbitrariedade colonial. Em 8 de novembro de 1965, as Ilhas Chagos, sob a soberania britânica desde 1810, após a cessão do arquipélago pela França, foram separadas de Maurício. Em 1968, Maurício conquistou a independência, embora parte de seu território tenha permanecido sob controle britânico. Na época, a ilha era habitada por cerca de 2.000 pessoas, a maioria descendentes de escravos de Madagascar e Moçambique e de trabalhadores da Índia.
Devido à sua posição estratégica, como parte da Guerra Fria, os Estados Unidos decidiram, em 1966, estabelecer uma base militar na ilha de Diego Garcia - a maior que Washington tem no exterior - por um total de 70 anos, até 2036. Para isso, em acordo com o governo britânico, todos os chagossianos, que viviam nessas terras desde o século 18, sem exceção, foram arbitrariamente deportados para Maurício e Seychelles entre 1966 e 1973, sem possibilidade de retorno até hoje. Essa é uma violação flagrante dos artigos 9, 13 e 17 da Declaração Universal dos Direitos Humanos de 1948. O artigo 9 afirma que “ninguém será submetido a prisão, detenção ou exílio arbitrários”. O artigo 13 afirma que “toda pessoa tem o direito de deixar qualquer país, inclusive o próprio, e de regressar ao seu país”. O artigo 17 afirma que “ninguém será arbitrariamente privado de seus bens”. Ainda mais grave, de acordo com o Artigo 7 do Estatuto de Roma do Tribunal Penal Internacional, “deportação ou transferência forçada de população” constitui um crime contra a humanidade.
Em notas confidenciais tingidas de racismo, nas quais os habitantes do arquipélago são chamados de “Tarzans” e “Fridays”, Londres reconheceu claramente a natureza ilegal da expulsão dos chagossianos, expressando seu desejo de apresentar às Nações Unidas “um fato consumado”. Por esse motivo, a comunidade internacional está exigindo justiça em nome do direito inalienável dos povos à autodeterminação, de acordo com a Carta das Nações Unidas. A Resolução 2066 da ONU, de 16 de dezembro de 1965, condenou o “desmembramento do território de Maurício” e a “violação de sua integridade territorial”. A Resolução 2232, de 20 de dezembro de 1965, estipula que “qualquer tentativa que vise à destruição parcial ou total da unidade nacional e da integridade territorial dos territórios coloniais e ao estabelecimento de bases e instalações militares nesses territórios é incompatível com os objetivos e princípios da Carta das Nações Unidas”.
O Movimento Não Alinhado, que reúne 120 nações, também está exigindo a devolução do território às Ilhas Maurício e o retorno dos chagossianos à sua terra natal. O Grupo de Estados da África, Caribe e Pacífico, formado por 79 países, considera a autoridade britânica sobre Chagos “ilegal” e pede a descolonização completa da África. A Comunidade de Desenvolvimento da África Austral, composta por 16 membros, pediu que o direito internacional e a Carta das Nações Unidas “prevaleçam” e que o arquipélago seja descolonizado.
Londres, apoiada por razões óbvias pelos Estados Unidos, recusa-se obstinadamente a respeitar os princípios elementares do direito internacional público e a Carta das Nações Unidas. De acordo com o governo britânico, a base militar é necessária “na luta contra os desafios mais complexos e urgentes do século XXI, como o terrorismo, o crime internacional, a pirataria ou qualquer outra forma de instabilidade”. No entanto, o povo chagossiano, que agora é forçado a viver na Inglaterra, nas Ilhas Maurício ou nas Ilhas Seychelles, não está pedindo o desmantelamento imediato da base, mas que exerça seu direito natural de viver em suas terras. O Reino Unido reconhece “que a maneira pela qual os chagossianos foram removidos do Arquipélago de Chagos e a maneira pela qual foram tratados posteriormente é vergonhosa e injusta”.
Em junho de 2017, de acordo com o Artigo 94 de sua Carta, a ONU adotou, por uma maioria de 94 votos contra 15, uma resolução solicitando um parecer consultivo da Corte Internacional de Justiça em Haia sobre a disputa entre Maurício e o Reino Unido. A ONU enfatizou as consequências sob o direito internacional “de manter o Arquipélago de Chagos sob a administração do Reino Unido, em particular no que diz respeito à impossibilidade de as Ilhas Maurício realizarem um programa de reassentamento para seus cidadãos, em particular os de origem chagossiana”.
Em 25 de fevereiro de 2019, em um parecer consultivo histórico, o Tribunal Internacional de Justiça enfatizou que o “desprendimento indevido do Arquipélago de Chagos” e sua “incorporação em uma nova colônia” impediram o processo tranquilo de descolonização das Ilhas Maurício. O Conselho de Segurança da ONU pediu que o Reino Unido “encerrasse sua administração do Arquipélago de Chagos o mais rápido possível”, instando todos os Estados membros a contribuírem para “a conclusão da descolonização de Maurício”.
Após essa decisão, em 22 de maio de 2019, a ONU adotou a resolução 73/295 por 116 votos a favor e seis contra, reconhecendo a soberania de Maurício sobre o Arquipélago de Chagos e exigindo a retirada incondicional da administração colonial britânica dentro de seis meses. De acordo com o texto, “como a separação do Arquipélago de Chagos não se baseou na expressão livre e genuína da vontade do povo mauriciano, a descolonização de Maurício não foi validamente concluída”. O documento pede que “nenhum impedimento ou obstáculo seja colocado no caminho do reassentamento” dos chagossianos em sua terra natal e rejeita “qualquer disposição feita pelo ‘Território Britânico do Oceano Índico’”. Até hoje, apesar de suas obrigações, o Reino Unido ainda se recusa a obedecer às regras do direito internacional e a respeitar as resoluções das Nações Unidas.
en_title: The Chagos Islands, the history of a crime against humanity
en_content:
For more than half a century, the Chagossian people have been fighting colonial arbitrariness. On 8 November 1965, the Chagos Islands, under British sovereignty since 1810, after the cession of the archipelago by France, were separated from Mauritius. In 1968, Mauritius gained independence, although part of its territory remained under British control. At the time, the island was inhabited by about 2,000 people, mostly descendants of slaves from Madagascar and Mozambique and workers from India.
Due to its strategic position, as part of the Cold War, the United States decided in 1966 to establish a military base on the island of Diego Garcia - the largest Washington has abroad - for a total of 70 years, up to 2036. For this, according to the British government, all the Chagossians, who lived in these lands since the 18th century, without exception, were arbitrarily deported to Mauritius and Seychelles between 1966 and 1973, without the possibility of returning to this day. This is a blatant violation of Articles 9, 13 and 17 of the Universal Declaration of Human Rights of 1948. Article 9 states that “no one will be subjected to arbitrary arrest, detention or exile.” Article 13 states that “every person has the right to leave any country, including himself, and to return to his country.” Article 17 states that “no one will be arbitrarily deprived of his goods.” Even more serious, according to Article 7 of the Statute of Rome of the International Criminal Court, “deportation or forced transfer of population” constitutes a crime against humanity.
In classified notes of racism, in which the inhabitants of the archipelago are called “Tarzans” and “Fridays”, London clearly recognized the illegal nature of the expulsion of the Chagossians, expressing their desire to present to the United Nations “a consummate fact”. For this reason, the international community is demanding justice in the name of the inalienable right of peoples to self-determination, according to the UN Charter. UN Resolution 2066 of 16 December 1965 condemned the "demembrance of the territory of Mauritius" and the "violation of its territorial integrity". Resolution 2232 of 20 December 1965 stipulates that “any attempt to partial or total destruction of national unity and territorial integrity of colonial territories and the establishment of military bases and facilities in these territories is incompatible with the goals and principles of the United Nations Charter”.
The Non-Aligned Movement, which brings together 120 nations, is also demanding the return of the territory to the Mauritius Islands and the return of the Chagossians to their homeland. The Group of African, Caribbean and Pacific States, formed by 79 countries, considers the British authority over Chagos “illegal” and calls for the complete decolonization of Africa. The Southern African Development Community, composed of 16 members, called for international law and the United Nations Charter to “prevalence” and for the archipelago to be decolonized.
London, supported for obvious reasons by the United States, refuses to respect the elementary principles of public international law and the United Nations Charter. According to the British government, the military base is necessary “in the fight against the most complex and urgent challenges of the 21st century, such as terrorism, international crime, piracy or any other form of instability.” However, the Chagossian people, who are now forced to live in England, the Mauritius Islands or the Seychelles, are not asking for the immediate dismantling of the base, but who exercise their natural right to live in their lands. The United Kingdom recognizes “that the way the Chagossians were removed from the Chagos Archipelago and the way they were subsequently treated is shameful and unfair.”
In June 2017, according to Article 94 of its Charter, the UN adopted, by a majority of 94 votes against 15, a resolution requesting an advisory opinion of the International Court of Justice in The Hague on the dispute between Mauritius and the United Kingdom. The UN emphasized the consequences under international law “to keep the Chagos Archipelago under the UK administration, in particular with regard to the impossibility of the Mauritius Islands to carry out a resettlement program for its citizens, in particular those of Chagossian origin.”
On February 25, 2019, in a historical advisory opinion, the International Court of Justice emphasized that the “indebted detachment of the Chagos Archipelago” and its “incorporation into a new colony” prevented the peaceful process of decolonization of the Mauritius Islands. The UN Security Council called on the United Kingdom to “enclose its administration of the Chagos Archipelago as soon as possible,” urging all member states to contribute to “the conclusion of the decolonization of Mauritius”.
After this decision, on May 22, 2019, the UN adopted resolution 73/295 for 116 votes in favour and six against, recognizing the sovereignty of Mauritius over the Chagos Archipelago and demanding the unconditional withdrawal of the British colonial administration within six months. According to the text, “as the separation of the Chagos Archipelago was not based on the free and genuine expression of the will of the Mauritian people, the decolonization of Mauritius was not validly completed”. The document asks that “no impediment or obstacle be placed on the path of resettlement” of the Chagossians in their homeland and rejects “any provision made by the British Indian Ocean Territory”. Even today, despite its obligations, the United Kingdom still refuses to comply with the rules of international law and to respect the UN resolutions.
en_subtitle: Between 1966 and 1973, the entire population of this archipelago in the Indian Ocean was deported from its homeland to allow the installation of a US military base.

title: Lei do TikTok é uma tentativa de censura, não é um aviso para as “Big Techs”
subtitle: Mas, ao misturar o tratamento legal dos EUA ao TikTok com o de sua própria indústria de tecnologia, a mídia obscurece as razões reais para a aprovação da lei
content:
À medida que a agitação dos legisladores dos EUA em relação ao TikTok culmina em uma lei que ameaça uma proibição nacional se a plataforma de mídia social não for vendida para um comprador dos EUA dentro de nove meses, uma narrativa midiática emergente encontra um ponto positivo. Cada movimento legislativo direcionado ao TikTok, diz a estória, tem o potencial de inspirar uma regulamentação muito necessária das gigantes da tecnologia como Meta, Amazon, Google e Apple.
Mas, ao misturar o tratamento legal dos EUA ao TikTok — uma subsidiária da ByteDance sediada em Pequim (China Popular) — com o de sua própria indústria de tecnologia, a mídia obscurece as razões reais para a aprovação da lei.
Isso ficou aparente em um artigo do New York Times (25/04/24) com o título "TikTok Quebrou o Emperramento Legal da Tecnologia. Esse Sucesso Pode Ser Repetido?" A autora Cecilia Kang descreveu a lei recentemente instaurada de desinvestimento ou proibição — aprovada como parte de um pacote com ajuda para Israel e Ucrânia — como uma instância de "domar as gigantes da tecnologia." O artigo sugeriu que a proibição poderia ser um prenúncio de uma regulamentação mais ampla da indústria de tecnologia no interesse público, como legislação antitruste ou salvaguardas de saúde mental.
Kang citou várias fontes que duvidavam que o ultimato estimularia a regulamentação das empresas de tecnologia dos EUA, argumentando que os legisladores influenciados por lobbies da indústria e estratégias de campanha de 2024 hesitariam em considerar a ideia de restringir o poder corporativo dos EUA.
É justo notar que a lei do TikTok provavelmente não teria esse efeito. Mas o lobby e a campanha não são as únicas, ou mesmo as principais, explicações para isso. Uma simples revisão da legislação mostra que não se trata de uma forma de regulamentação de boa fé destinada a proteger a população, mas um esforço para apreender ou enfraquecer severamente o TikTok em nome dos interesses dos EUA.
A tese de Kang foi baseada em anos de temores divulgados pela mídia e pelos formuladores de políticas de que os dados dos usuários do TikTok estavam suscetíveis a vigilância pelo governo chinês (BuzzFeed News, 17/06/22; Forbes, 20/10/22; Guardian, 07/11/22). Segundo colegas de Kang, a promulgação da lei foi motivada por "preocupações de que o governo chinês pudesse acessar dados sensíveis do usuário" (New York Times, 26/04/24). Em 2023, o governador de Montana, Greg Gianforte, tentou proibir o TikTok em todo o seu estado com base no argumento de que "o Partido Comunista Chinês" estava "coletando informações pessoais, privadas e sensíveis dos usuários dos EUA" (Montana Free Press, 17/05/23). (A tentativa de Gianforte foi posteriormente frustrada por um juiz federal.)
Se tais temores fossem a motivação genuína das autoridades, poderíamos esperar que uma regulamentação mais ampla de privacidade de dados se seguisse. No entanto, como o Times deixou de mencionar, as acusações de espionagem são tênues — e profundamente cínicas. Como até mesmo os oficiais de inteligência dos EUA concedem, os receios sobre o acesso da China aos dados dos usuários do TikTok são estritamente hipotéticos (Intercept, 16/03/24). E, apesar de sua manchete bombástica "Análise: Agora Há Algumas Evidências Públicas de que a China Visualizou Dados do TikTok", a CNN (08/06/23) advertiu que tais evidências — um depoimento juramentado de um ex-funcionário da ByteDance — "permanecem bastante frágeis".
Quanto ao pretexto para censura, dada sua natureza duvidosa, é difícil ver essas reivindicações de privacidade de dados como qualquer coisa além de um pretexto para os EUA sufocarem o TikTok. Ao forçar o desinvestimento ou a proibição, os EUA, pelo menos teoricamente, ganham o seguinte: transferem uma empresa tremendamente lucrativa e influente para as suas próprias mãos, ou impedem que essa empresa sirva como uma plataforma — embora com muitos problemas — na qual as pessoas podem se envolver e aprender com discursos críticos ao império dos EUA.
As intenções censoriais da legislação foram evidenciadas pelos republicanos no Congresso. Em um discurso em 24 de abril, o dia em que o presidente Joe Biden promulgou a lei do ultimato, o senador Pete Ricketts (R–Neb.) lamentou que "quase um terço" dos usuários entre 18 e 29 anos usem o TikTok como fonte regular de notícias. (Resultados de uma pesquisa do Pew de novembro de 2023 confirmam isso.) Isso foi motivo de alarme, segundo o senador, porque a plataforma apresentava uma concentração elevada de vídeos "pró-Palestina e pró-Hamas" como parte de um plano malévolo do governo chinês.
O senador e ex-candidato presidencial Mitt Romney (R–Utah) reforçou o medo de Ricketts no início de maio, afirmando em um fórum com o Secretário de Estado Antony Blinken que "o número de menções aos palestinos" no TikTok gerava "um apoio avassalador para fechá-lo" (Common Dreams, 06/05/24). A fonte de Romney para isso não estava clara, mas sua mensagem era: o TikTok simplesmente não seria tolerado como uma fonte de informação que contradiga as narrativas oficiais.
Da mesma forma, o deputado Mike Lawler (R–NY) (Intercept, 04/05/24) disse ao grupo de defesa centrista 'No Labels' que os protestos em Gaza são exatamente a razão pela qual incluímos o projeto de lei do TikTok no pacote de ajuda suplementar estrangeira, porque estamos vendo como esses jovens estão sendo manipulados por certos grupos ou entidades ou países para fomentar o ódio em seu nome e realmente criar um ambiente hostil aqui nos EUA.
Os legisladores de direita estavam longe de serem os primeiros a abrigar esse sentimento; críticas como essas vinham fervendo há meses (FAIR.org, 13/11/23, 14/03/24). (Essas admissões de que o Congresso foi atrás do TikTok com base em seu conteúdo provavelmente ajudarão a ação judicial que a ByteDance entrou argumentando que a lei que exige a venda ou a proibição é inconstitucional — Hollywood Reporter, 07/05/24).
Ignorando esse contexto, a Associated Press (24/03/24) apresentou as mesmas caracterizações incorretas que o New York Times. Parafraseando o senador Mark Warner (D-Va.), a AP relatou que a lei do TikTok — que, na época, era apenas um projeto de lei aprovado pela Câmara — "é a melhor chance de conseguir algo depois de anos de inação" na regulamentação da tecnologia. O conteúdo moral do que, exatamente, estava sendo feito não parecia importar para a agência de notícias. Em vez disso, a AP optou por publicar sem críticas a insinuação de Warner de que os jovens usuários do TikTok, instando seus representantes congressuais a votar contra a proibição, foram "manipulados" pelo "Partido Comunista da China".
O relatório da AP ecoou um resumo igualmente falho da NBC News (16/04/23) sobre abordagens congressuais para a indústria de tecnologia. Embora o artigo tenha sido publicado antes de qualquer legislação do TikTok, ele comentou sobre um "grande impulso bipartidário" para "reprimir" a empresa. O artigo continuou agrupando o que na época era um movimento mais abstrato — mas totalmente jingoísta — contra o TikTok com regulamentações relacionadas à proteção do usuário, como deep fakes, golpes de phishing por voz e chatbots poderosos como o Chat GPT.
 - Ausente desses relatórios está mais uma razão pela qual uma proibição ou venda forçada do TikTok não levará necessariamente à regulamentação doméstica: as gigantes da tecnologia dos EUA têm a ganhar com a lei. Como o próprio New York Times (24/04/24) relata, "a Meta poderia arrecadar até 60% da receita de publicidade americana do TikTok, enquanto o YouTube poderia levar mais cerca de 25%." Não coincidentemente, pelo menos uma empresa de tecnologia dos EUA esteve envolvida na fabricação da antipatia pública em relação ao TikTok: de acordo com o Washington Post (30/03/22), a Meta, concorrente direta do TikTok, pagou a uma firma de consultoria republicana para orquestrar uma campanha difamatória contra o TikTok. O esforço incluiu artigos de opinião plantados e cartas ao editor em "importantes veículos de notícias regionais" em todo o país.
Acoplando essas informações com a recusa histórica dos EUA em regular a sua própria indústria de tecnologia, poderíamos nos perguntar por que os EUA mudariam repentinamente de curso? E isso não significaria que um TikTok de propriedade dos EUA operaria efetivamente sem controle, assim como as atuais corporações de tecnologia dos EUA?
Mas tais perguntas não são feitas em uma narrativa que “lava” a política reacionária com uma potencial vantagem regulatória. O ultimato do TikTok, nos dizem, não é uma medida drástica para sufocar declarações de apoio à Palestina ou qualquer outro discurso político à esquerda da linha do Departamento de Estado; tomando emprestando do New York Times (25/04/24), o ultimato é um "sucesso".
en_title: TikTok’s law is an attempt to censor, not a warning to the “Big Techs”
en_content:
As the agitation of U.S. legislators in relation to TikTok culminates in a law that threatens a national prohibition if the social media platform is not sold to a U.S. buyer within nine months, an emerging media narrative finds a positive point. Each legislative movement directed at TikTok, says the story, has the potential to inspire a much needed regulation of technology giants like Meta, Amazon, Google and Apple.
But by mixing the legal treatment of the US to TikTok — a subsidiary of ByteDance based in Beijing (China Popular) — with that of its own technology industry, the media obscures the real reasons for law approval.
This became apparent in a New York Times article (25/04/24) with the title "TikTok Broken the Legal Emperement of Technology. Can this Success Be Repeated?" Author Cecilia Kang described the recently introduced law of divestment or prohibition—approved as part of a package with aid to Israel and Ukraine—as an instance of "doing the giants of technology." The article suggested that the prohibition could be a provision of wider regulation of the technology industry in the public interest, such as antitrust legislation or mental health safeguards.
Kang cited several sources that doubted that the ultimatum would stimulate the regulation of U.S. technology companies, arguing that industry lobby-influenced legislators and 2024 campaign strategies would hesitate to consider restricting U.S. corporate power.
It is fair to note that the law of TikTok probably would not have that effect. But the lobby and the campaign are not the only, or even the main, explanations for it. A simple review of legislation shows that it is not a form of regulation of good faith intended to protect the population, but an effort to apprehend or severely weaken TikTok in the name of US interests.
The Kang thesis was based on years of fears disclosed by the media and policy makers that the data of TikTok users were susceptible to surveillance by the Chinese government (BuzzFeed News, 17/06/22; Forbes, 20/10/22; Guardian, 07/11/22). According to Kang's colleagues, the enactment of the law was motivated by "concerns that the Chinese government could access user sensitive data" (New York Times, 26/04/24). In 2023, the governor of Montana, Greg Gianforte, attempted to forbid TikTok throughout his state based on the argument that "the Chinese Communist Party" was "collecting personal, private and sensitive information from U.S. users" (Montana Free Press, 17/05/23). (Gianforte's attempt was later frustrated by a federal judge.)
If such fears were the genuine motivation of the authorities, we could expect wider data privacy regulations to follow. However, as the Times stopped mentioning, the charges of espionage are tenuous — and deeply cynical. As even U.S. intelligence officers grant, China's fears of access to data from TikTok users are strictly hypothetical (Intercept, 16/03/24). And in spite of its "Analysis: Now there are some Public Evidences that China has Visualized Data from TikTok", CNN (08/06/23) warned that such evidence — a sworn testimony of a former employee of ByteDance — "they remain quite fragile".
As for the pretext for censorship, given its dubious nature, it is difficult to see these data privacy claims as anything but a pretext for the US to suffocate TikTok. By forcing divestment or prohibition, the US, at least theoretically, gains the following: transfer a tremendously lucrative and influential company to its own hands, or prevent this company from serving as a platform—although with many problems—in which people can engage and learn from critical speeches to the US empire.
The censorship intentions of the legislation were evidenced by the Republicans in Congress. In a speech on April 24, the day President Joe Biden promulgated the ultimatum law, Senator Pete Ricketts (R–Neb.) regretted that "almost a third" of users between 18 and 29 years old use TikTok as a regular news source. (Results of a Pew survey of November 2023 confirm this.) This was a cause of alarm, according to the senator, because the platform presented a high concentration of "pro-Palestine and pro-Hamas" videos as part of a Chinese government evil plan.
Senator and former presidential candidate Mitt Romney (R–Utah) reinforced the fear of Ricketts in early May, stating in a forum with Secretary of State Antony Blinken that "the number of mentions to the Palestinians" in TikTok generated "an overwhelming support to close it" (Common Dreams, 06/05/24). Romney's source for this was unclear, but his message was: TikTok would simply not be tolerated as a source of information that contradicts the official narratives.
Similarly, Deputy Mike Lawler (R–NY) (Intercept, 04/05/24) told the centrist defence group 'No Labels' that the protests in Gaza are exactly the reason why we included the TikTok bill in the foreign supplementary aid package, because we are seeing how these young people are being manipulated by certain groups or entities or countries to foster hatred on their behalf and actually create a hostile environment here in the US.
The right-wing legislators were far from being the first to shelter this feeling; criticisms such as these were boiling for months (FAIR.org, 13/11/23, 14/03/24). (These admissions that Congress went after TikTok on the basis of its content will likely help the judicial action that ByteDance filed arguing that the law that requires sale or prohibition is unconstitutional — Hollywood Reporter, 07/05/24).
Ignoring this context, the Associated Press (24/03/24) presented the same incorrect features as the New York Times. Paraphrasing Senator Mark Warner (D-Va.), the AP reported that the law of TikTok — which at the time was only a bill approved by the House — "is the best chance of getting something after years of inaction" in technology regulation. The moral content of what exactly was being done didn’t seem to matter to the news agency. Instead, the AP chose to publish without criticism Warner's insinuation that the young users of TikTok, urging their congressional representatives to vote against the ban, were "manipulated" by the "Communist Party of China".
The AP report echoed an equally flawed summary of NBC News (16/04/23) on congressional approaches to the technology industry. Although the article was published before any TikTok legislation, he commented on a "bipartisan boost" to "repress" the company. The article continued to group what at the time was a more abstract movement — but totally jingoist — against TikTok with regulations related to user protection such as deep fakes, voice phishing scams and powerful chatbots like Chat GPT.
- Absent of these reports is another reason why a forced prohibition or sale of TikTok will not necessarily lead to domestic regulation: US technology giants have to win with the law. As the New York Times itself (24/04/24) reports, "Meta could raise up to 60% of TikTok's American advertising revenue, while YouTube could take over 25%." Not coincidentally, at least one U.S. technology company was involved in the manufacture of public antipathy with TikTok: according to the Washington Post (30/03/22), TikTok’s direct competitor Meta paid a Republican consulting firm to orchestrate a defamatory campaign against TikTok. The effort included articles of opinion planted and letters to the editor in "important regional news vehicles" across the country.
By engaging this information with the US historical refusal to regulate its own technology industry, could we wonder why the US would suddenly change course? And wouldn't that mean that a U.S. owned TikTok would effectively operate without control, as well as the current U.S. technology corporations?
But such questions are not asked in a narrative that “washes” reactionary politics with a potential regulatory advantage. TikTok's ultimatum, they tell us, is not a drastic measure to stifle statements of support to Palestine or any other political discourse to the left of the State Department line; borrowing from the New York Times (25/04/24), the ultimatum is a "success".
en_subtitle: But by mixing the legal treatment of the US to TikTok with that of its own technology industry, the media obscures the real reasons for law approval

title: A era de impunidade para os tomadores de decisão israelenses acabou
subtitle: A comunidade internacional está sinalizando para Israel que não pode mais manter a sua política de violência, morte e destruição sem responsabilidade
content:
O pedido do procurador do Tribunal Penal Internacional (TPI) para emitir mandados de prisão contra o primeiro-ministro israelense Benjamin Netanyahu e o ministro da Defesa Yoav Gallant por suspeita de cometer crimes de guerra em Gaza sinaliza o rápido declínio de Israel em um abismo moral.
A comunidade internacional está sinalizando para Israel que não pode mais manter a sua política de violência, morte e destruição sem responsabilidade. Da mesma forma, o pedido de mandados de prisão contra líderes do Hamas pelos crimes de 7 de outubro é importante e traça uma linha vermelha onde o dano a civis está em questão.
Dada a escalada da violência, é nosso dever afirmar claramente: o regime israelense que controla o território entre o Rio Jordão e o Mar Mediterrâneo não pode, e não quer, investigar seus crimes contra os palestinos. Por décadas, Israel tem usado seu sistema de aplicação da lei doméstica como um mecanismo de dissimulação para desviar críticas internacionais. “O sistema não investiga as ordens em si, nem a responsabilidade daqueles que as emitem ou que determinam a política”, afirmou a B'Tselem em seu relatório “
A violência israelense dirigida aos palestinos para perpetuar a ocupação é violência estatal e é inerente ao regime de apartheid israelense. Isso é verdade na Cisjordânia, na Faixa de Gaza e em todos os outros lugares. A intervenção do TPI e as decisões do CIJ são uma chance para nós, israelenses, percebermos o que deveríamos ter entendido há muito tempo: que manter um regime de supremacia, violência e opressão envolve necessariamente crimes e graves violações dos direitos humanos.
Apelamos à comunidade internacional para agir urgentemente para conseguir um cessar-fogo e um acordo para a libertação de todos os reféns, a fim de parar o derramamento de sangue e a catástrofe humanitária.
en_title: The era of impunity for Israeli decision makers is over
en_content:
The request of the Attorney General of the International Criminal Court (ICT) to issue arrest warrants against Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu and Defense Minister Yoav Gallant suspected of committing war crimes in Gaza signals the rapid decline of Israel in a moral abyss.
The international community is signaling to Israel that it can no longer maintain its policy of violence, death and destruction without responsibility. Similarly, the request for arrest warrants against Hamas leaders for the crimes of October 7 is important and traces a red line where the damage to civilians is in question.
Given the escalation of violence, it is our duty to state clearly: the Israeli regime that controls the territory between the Jordan River and the Mediterranean Sea cannot, and does not want to investigate its crimes against the Palestinians. For decades, Israel has used its domestic law enforcement system as a dissimulation mechanism to divert international criticism. “The system does not investigate the orders in itself, nor the responsibility of those who issue them or who determine the policy”, said B’Tselem in his report “
Israeli violence directed to the Palestinians to perpetuate occupation is state violence and is inherent in the Israeli apartheid regime. This is true in the West Bank, the Gaza Strip and everywhere else. ICC intervention and ICJ decisions are a chance for us, Israelis, to realize what we should have understood long ago: that maintaining a regime of supremacy, violence and oppression necessarily involves crimes and serious human rights violations.
We call on the international community to act urgently to achieve a ceasefire and an agreement for the release of all hostages in order to stop bloodshed and humanitarian catastrophe.
en_subtitle: The international community is signaling to Israel that it can no longer maintain its policy of violence, death and destruction without responsibility

title: Gaza, a falência moral do Ocidente
subtitle: O drama que se desenrola em Gaza tem uma causa: a colonização e a ocupação da Palestina por Israel, o regime de apartheid infligido à população e o bloqueio imposto desde 2007
content:
Ajudando e incentivando o governo supremacista de Benjamin Netanyahu por meio de seu apoio material e político, os países ocidentais foram desacreditados aos olhos da opinião pública mundial, que está testemunhando com consternação o genocídio cometido contra o povo palestino.
Desde os massacres de 7 de outubro de 2023 perpetrados pelo Hamas, que ceifaram a vida de 400 soldados e 800 civis, um dilúvio de fogo de barbaridade indescritível tem chovido continuamente sobre o povo de Gaza, que foi abandonado à própria sorte.
